feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

paragraphs.text: As European policymakers consider the possibility of strengthening consumer protection and digital fairness through the upcoming Digital Fairness Act, much focus has fallen on addictive design and features that encourage prolonged users' engagement. We acknowledge that such features may raise concerns when deployed in ways that prioritise engagement over wellbeing or productivity.
Yet these same features, in a different context, benefit consumers by helping them achieve their goals:
Millions of European consumers have been able to reach running and cycling goals, thanks to Strava's motivational features helping them commit to regular exercise.
For example, a simple notification on Strava informing that a friend has just finished a run can nudge a hesitant runner out the door, turning intention into action.
Millions of European consumers have been able to learn another language, thanks to Duolingo's motivational features helping them commit to regular learning.
For example, the Duolingo "streak" encourages users to regularly practice, something critical to language learning.
Users who maintain a Duolingo streak demonstrate significantly greater learning and higher proficiency than users who do not.

Users turn to apps like Strava or Duolingo because they aspire to reach goals such as reaching their fitness goals (e.g. running a marathon) or learning a new language. However, achieving these ambitions can be challenging and often requires consistent daily practice. The process of habit formation follows predictable patterns. Wood's (2019) extensive research (spanning three decades) reveals that habits
are formed through repeated performance of behaviors in consistent contexts, eventually becoming automatic responses that require minimal conscious effort to maintain.

Engagement features, including rewards and gamification, play a valuable role in helping users stay motivated and committed to these goals. Without such support, many users may find it difficult to maintain progress or build lasting habits. The role that engagement features play in self-improvement is also recognised by independent academic research. For example, Li et al. (2024) highlight that these features promote the formation of healthy habits and help individuals sustain improvements over time by encouraging repetition, offering motivational nudges and helping track progress.

Apps also provide real-time feedback (a completed run summary or immediate correction on a quiz), contextual nudges (a reminder at your usual workout or study time), and social reinforcement (community kudos or leaderboards). Singh (2024) shows that this feedback is especially important in the fragile early stages of habit formation, and Gera et al. (2024) demonstrates that reinforcing behaviours in controlled settings accelerates habit development.

In other words, consumer welfare depends not on the features themselves but on the outcome they encourage. Engagement features designed and used primarily to encourage healthy habits or achieve aspirations do not present the same problems as those used to encourage short-term, easy, passive habits to maximise time spent on apps for monetisation purposes.

Purposeful platforms like Strava and Duolingo use such features to encourage challenging tasks, like an early morning workout. The very nature of that activity deters abuse; users rarely regret how much time they accidentally spent on verb conjugation, particularly when wanting to learn more was the reason they downloaded the app in the first place.

The willingness toward encouraging purposeful self-improvement is also reflected in our business models built on long-term subscriptions and not advertising. It aligns our incentives with our users: we are successful only if our users think we play a positive role in their lives.

Accordingly, we encourage policymakers to consider key variables when assessing potential risks and harm related to apps' engaging features including: purpose, magnitude, interaction, and commercial incentives/ monetisation.
First, purpose. Is the feature encouraging a user-defined, beneficial goal (e.g., education or fitness) or merely maximising time spent?
Users download purposeful platforms like Strava and Duolingo because they want to spend more time exercising or learning a language.
In that context, engagement features that nudge users towards running more or learning more are achieving the intended aims of the user and promoting consumer benefit.
Second, magnitude. What is the typical session length/daily duration, and is there evidence of "overuse" (e.g., high regret rates)?
On average, people spend only minutes per day on Strava or Duolingo - unlikely to create significant harm.

More importantly, the time they spend is rarely 'regretted'. In a recent survey of EU users, more than 95% of respondents considered their Duolingo screen time to be 'healthy', and more than 98% considered it to be 'productive'.

The low amounts of time highlight the challenge that purposeful platforms face: it's hard to convince people to spend time learning or exercising, even among users self-selecting into downloading such apps in the first place.
Third, interaction. Are sessions passive, or do they require active user engagement?
For example, Duolingo lessons require active user engagement and conscious learning. Likewise, Strava sessions are strongly correlated with time spent exercising.
Fourth, commercial incentives/monetisation. Are business incentives aligned with user success (e.g., subscriptions tied to outcomes)?
Purposeful platforms succeed only if their users succeed in achieving those goals. As such, their incentives are strongly aligned with their users.
For example, neither Strava's nor Duolingo's business model is built on monetising screen time; instead, both business models rely on users committing to their goals through long-term subscriptions.

It is therefore important for policymakers, when considering addictive design, to distinguish between apps that use engagement features to support users in building positive, sustainable habits, and those that employ such features primarily to maximise engagement for commercial purposes. Without this distinction and safe harbour, any upcoming legislation risks penalising platforms that actively reinforce self-directed goals such as learning a language, exercising regularly, or maintaining mental wellbeing, in line with the Commission's objectives of encouraging lifelong learning, and delivering opportunities for mental and physical wellbeing for everyone.

Strava and Duolingo remain committed to supporting the goals of the Digital Fairness Act and welcome continued dialogue with policymakers. Both companies are open to sharing further insights on how engagement features can be designed to promote user wellbeing, motivation, and long-term success in achieving personal goals.
